title: Thyroid Imbalance
tagline: The butterfly gland that controls everything
oneLiner: Your thyroid gland produces hormones that control your metabolism, energy, mood, weight, and heart rate.

cards.title: What is the Thyroid?
cards.body: The thyroid is a small butterfly-shaped gland in your neck. It makes hormones (T3 and T4) that set the speed of almost every process in your body — like the volume knob for your metabolism.

cards.title: Hypothyroidism (Under-active)
cards.body: When the thyroid makes too little hormone, everything slows down. You feel tired, gain weight, feel cold, have dry skin, and can become constipated. Very common in Indian women.

cards.title: Hyperthyroidism (Over-active)
cards.body: Too much thyroid hormone speeds everything up — rapid heartbeat, weight loss despite eating well, anxiety, sweating, and trembling hands.

cards.title: Iodine is the Building Block
cards.body: Thyroid hormones are made from iodine. Too little iodine causes a goitre (swollen thyroid). India's iodised salt programme was created to fix this — use iodised salt, not rock salt, in daily cooking.

cards.title: Medicine Timing is Critical
cards.body: Thyroid medicine (like Thyroxine) must be taken on an empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before food. Taking it with tea, coffee, or milk blocks absorption and the medicine won't work properly.

cards.title: Raw Goitrogenic Foods
cards.body: Raw cabbage, cauliflower, broccoli, and soy contain compounds that can interfere with thyroid hormone production — especially if iodine is low. Cooking destroys most of this effect.

cards.title: TSH Test Explained
cards.body: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone) is produced by the brain. High TSH = thyroid is under-active (brain is shouting to get it working). Low TSH = thyroid is over-active. Normal TSH is 0.4–4.0 mIU/L.

doEat.name: Brazil nuts (1–2/day)
doEat.note: Extremely rich in selenium (needed for T4→T3 conversion)

doEat.name: Curd & dairy
doEat.note: Iodine-rich; take away from thyroid medicine

dontEat.name: Raw cabbage / kale in large amounts
dontEat.note: Goitrogens block hormone production — cook it

dontEat.name: Unfermented soy (tofu, soy milk) in excess
Gluten (if Hashimoto's suspected)
dontEat.note: Autoimmune thyroid disease may be gluten-triggered

lifestyle: Take thyroid medicine first thing in the morning, 30–60 minutes before breakfast or chai.
Get a TSH test at least once a year (more often if on medication or dose changes).
Exercise regularly — even a 30-minute walk improves metabolism and reduces hypothyroid fatigue.
Manage stress — cortisol suppresses thyroid hormone conversion (T4→T3).
Don't self-adjust medicine dose — always consult your doctor when you change it.
Use iodised salt in your daily cooking. Switch from rock salt (sendha namak) which has no iodine.
redFlags: Extreme fatigue, swollen face, or puffiness around eyes that isn't going away
Heart palpitations or rapid heartbeat at rest — may be hyperthyroidism
Large swelling on the front of the neck (goitre)
Sudden unexplained weight gain or loss of 5+ kg over a month
Mental confusion or memory problems in an older person (can be thyroid-related)

quiz.why: High TSH means the brain is 'shouting' at the thyroid to work harder — a sign the thyroid is under-active.

quiz.q: Thyroid medicine should be taken with morning tea.
quiz.why: Tea, coffee, and milk block absorption of thyroxine. Always take it on an empty stomach 30–60 min before food.

quiz.q: Which nutrient is the main building block of thyroid hormones?
quiz.why: T3 and T4 hormones are literally made from iodine atoms. No iodine = no thyroid hormone.

quiz.q: Cooking cabbage or cauliflower makes it safer for thyroid patients.
quiz.why: Heat destroys most goitrogenic compounds in cruciferous vegetables — cooking them makes them safe to eat.

options: Unexplained weight gain and always feeling cold
quiz.why: Under-active thyroid slows metabolism — causing weight gain, cold intolerance, fatigue, and dry skin.
